title: Efficacy of Smartphone-Based Mindfulness Applications for Anxiety Reduction in Adolescents
research_question: Do unguided smartphone-based mindfulness and meditation applications reduce anxiety symptoms compared to standard care or inactive digital controls in adolescents?

pico.population: adolescents (ages 12 to 18 years) exhibiting mild to moderate anxiety symptoms, generalized anxiety, or elevated stress levels
pico.intervention: unguided, smartphone-based digital health interventions focusing on mindfulness, meditation, or cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) principles
pico.comparator: waitlist control, treatment as usual (TAU), or inactive digital controls (e.g., educational apps, passive tracking apps)
pico.outcome: reduction in anxiety symptoms measured by validated psychological scales (e.g., GAD-7, SCAS), user adherence rates, and self-reported well-being
pico.study_design: randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and quasi-experimental study designs

inclusion_criteria.text: Study must focus on adolescent populations strictly between the ages of 12 and 18.
Intervention must be delivered primarily via a mobile application (smartphone or tablet) without mandatory real-time human therapist interaction.
Study must report quantitative changes in anxiety or stress using validated psychological measurement tools.
Study must include a distinct control or comparison group (waitlist, standard care, or placebo app).

exclusion_criteria.text: Studies evaluating in-person therapy, telehealth video sessions, or web-based interventions requiring a desktop computer.
Populations with severe, clinically diagnosed psychiatric disorders requiring inpatient treatment or pharmacological intervention.
Studies involving concurrent pharmacological interventions where the effect of the app cannot be isolated.

title: Impact of Sugar-Sweetened Beverage Taxes on Population Obesity and BMI
research_question: Do regional or national taxes on sugar-sweetened beverages significantly reduce population-level obesity rates or average Body Mass Index (BMI) compared to jurisdictions without such taxes?

pico.population: general human populations (children, adolescents, or adults) living in municipalities, regions, or countries where a sugar-sweetened beverage tax has been implemented
pico.intervention: implementation of a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) tax, soda tax, or sugary drink excise tax
pico.comparator: populations in similar jurisdictions without an SSB tax, or the exact same population in the period immediately prior to the tax implementation
pico.outcome: changes in the prevalence of obesity, prevalence of overweight status, or changes in mean Body Mass Index (BMI)
pico.study_design: natural experiments, interrupted time series (ITS), difference-in-differences (DiD), and large-scale prospective cohort studies

inclusion_criteria.text: Study must evaluate a legally implemented, real-world tax on sugar-sweetened beverages.
Study must report quantitative changes in human anthropometric outcomes (e.g., BMI, body weight, obesity prevalence, or adiposity).
Study must use a quasi-experimental, observational cohort, or natural experiment study design with a defined pre-tax and post-tax evaluation period.

exclusion_criteria.text: Studies that only measure beverage purchasing behavior, sales volume, or economic impact without measuring actual human weight or health outcomes.
Mathematical modeling or simulation studies projecting future health impacts (must be empirical post-implementation data).
Clinical weight-loss trials or dietary interventions at the individual level (e.g., counseling someone to stop drinking soda).
